one_line_takeaway: Freezing the CLIP backbone and inserting lightweight residual TokenRefiner modules at every transformer layer of a multi-modal, multi-layer prompt-tuning defense purifies adversarially distorted visual and textual token features directly, beating prior prompt-tuning defenses by roughly 31-32 percentage points under AutoAttack while avoiding the catastrophic clean-accuracy collapse that full backbone fine-tuning (TeCoA) suffers.
key_finding: On the (own) rigorous AutoAttack white-box benchmark (epsilon=1/255), NAP-Tuning reaches 48.9% average robust accuracy on ViT-B/16 and 44.0% on ViT-B/32 across the 11 datasets, which the paper reports as outperforming the strongest baselines by 32.3% (ViT-B16) and 31.3% (ViT-B32) -- reading Table II/III, the next-best baseline under AutoAttack is FAP at 16.1% (ViT-B16, avg) and 12.7% (ViT-B32, avg), so NAP-Tuning's absolute-percentage-point lead over the strongest baseline is roughly 32.8pp (48.9-16.1) and 31.3pp (44.0-12.7) respectively -- consistent with the paper's claimed magnitude even though the paper labels these as relative '%' improvements. Under the black-box M-Attack (epsilon=8/255) NAP-Tuning beats the strongest baseline by 16.4pp on ViT-B16 (58.8% vs. AdvPT-VLI's 41.9%) and 13.9pp on ViT-B32 (53.7% vs. AdvPT-V's 39.8%). Clean accuracy is competitive but slightly below AdvPT (e.g., 73.5% vs. AdvPT's 73.9% avg on ViT-B16). Feature-distortion analysis (Table V, L2 norm of Eq. 9) shows NAP-Tuning achieves the lowest distortion (1.96) versus Vanilla CLIP (8.67), AdvPT (8.63), APD (5.01), AdvPT-V (4.87), AdvPT-VLI (4.76), and FAP (3.58). Ablations show the TokenRefiner is not just an enhancement but a stability prerequisite: removing it (0 layers) causes training collapse under 5-step PGD adversarial training (near-random accuracy), while 2 layers resolves the instability and further depth saturates. Combining NAP-Tuning with the external DiffPure image-purification defense yields a further average 12.9 percentage-point improvement over standalone NAP-Tuning, demonstrating the model-side and input-side defenses are complementary. Compared to full fine-tuning (TeCoA) on ViT-B32/PGD-100, TeCoA achieves a smaller clean-vs-robust gap (15.3% vs. NAP-Tuning's 24.2%) but suffers a 32.3-point clean-accuracy collapse (69.1% to 36.8%), illustrating the catastrophic-forgetting cost of unfreezing the backbone that NAP-Tuning avoids.
core_contribution: NAP-Tuning is a journal extension of the authors' own prior conference work AdvPT (Adversarial Prompt Tuning). The paper's own new contribution (explicitly distinguished from AdvPT and from other cited prompt-tuning defenses like FAP and APD) is threefold: (1) extending prompt tuning from text-only to a coordinated multi-modal (visual + textual) and multi-layer (all 12 transformer layers) prompting architecture; (2) introducing a 'Neural Augmentor' module built from lightweight two-layer 'TokenRefiner' networks with residual connections that operate directly on intermediate token feature representations (both visual and textual) to explicitly purify/rectify adversarially distorted features, rather than only aligning input-side prompt vectors as prior prompt-tuning defenses do; and (3) a principled two-term training loss (clean loss + a sigmoid-scheduled dynamically weighted adversarial loss, Eq. 14-17) replacing the more heuristic multi-term losses of prior work. The backbone (CLIP image/text encoders) remains frozen throughout; only the prompts and TokenRefiner parameters are trained.
threat_model: The paper explicitly adopts a stronger white-box/black-box threat model than its own prior AdvPT work. Whereas AdvPT (cited as reference [3], the authors' own earlier paper) assumed the attacker only has access to the image encoder and maximizes KL divergence between clean/adversarial image features (Eq. 2), NAP-Tuning assumes the attacker has access to both image and text encoders (including the textual prompt) and crafts adversarial images by maximizing cross-entropy loss over the full image-text similarity (Eq. 3). Perturbations are L_p-bounded (l_infinity in training/PGD/AutoAttack evaluation, epsilon = 1/255 for white-box PGD-100 and AutoAttack; epsilon = 8/255 for the black-box M-Attack, described in the paper as a deliberately larger budget because black-box attacks are inherently weaker). Attacks evaluated: PGD-100 and AutoAttack (white-box), and M-Attack (black-box, transfer-based). Training-time adversarial examples are generated with 5-step PGD (epsilon = 1/255 default, with epsilon in {1,2,4} px/255 explored in Table VI). The model itself is the target (CLIP zero-shot image classification); there is no LLM-agent, prompt-injection, or generative-content threat model in this paper -- it is purely adversarial-example robustness for a contrastive VLM classifier.
limitations: The paper is entirely self-reported/self-benchmarked with no external replication in the visible text. Training-time perturbation budget is fixed at a fairly small epsilon=1/255 for the primary results (Tables II/III), and Table VI shows that when tested at a much larger epsilon=8, robust accuracy collapses steeply regardless of training epsilon (e.g., trained at epsilon=4, robust accuracy falls to 11.8% at test epsilon=8), indicating limited robustness generalization to larger perturbation budgets. The method is evaluated only on CLIP-style contrastive VLMs (ViT-B/16, ViT-B/32); no results are given for generative/LLM-based VLMs (e.g., LLaVA, Qwen-VL) despite these being mentioned in Related Work. Few-shot (16-shot) training regime is explicitly noted by the authors themselves to introduce a mild overfitting tendency (inverted-U-shaped accuracy vs. prompt-depth curves on complex datasets like ImageNet/Food101/SUN397). No adaptive-attack evaluation against the specific TokenRefiner mechanism itself is reported (attacks used -- PGD, AutoAttack, M-Attack -- are the standard suite, not attacks custom-designed to target the purification module). Computational overhead is nontrivial: NAP-Tuning trains at ~2.9x the wall-clock time of the lightweight text-only AdvPT baseline (99 vs. 34 minutes on Pets), though still cheaper than full fine-tuning (TeCoA, 3.62x).

quantitative_results: AutoAttack robust accuracy (avg over 11 datasets), ViT-B16: NAP-Tuning 48.9% vs strongest baseline FAP 16.1%
AutoAttack robust accuracy (avg), ViT-B32: NAP-Tuning 44.0% vs strongest baseline FAP 12.7%
M-Attack (black-box, eps=8/255) robust accuracy (avg), ViT-B16: NAP-Tuning 58.8% vs strongest baseline AdvPT-VLI 41.9% (+16.4pp)
M-Attack robust accuracy (avg), ViT-B32: NAP-Tuning 53.7% vs strongest baseline AdvPT-V 39.8% (+13.9pp)
Clean accuracy (avg), ViT-B16: NAP-Tuning 73.5% vs AdvPT 73.9% (best)
Feature distortion L2 norm (Table V): Vanilla 8.67, AdvPT 8.63, APD 5.01, AdvPT-V 4.87, AdvPT-VLI 4.76, FAP 3.58, NAP-Tuning 1.96 (lowest/best)
TeCoA vs NAP-Tuning (ViT-B32, PGD-100): TeCoA clean-robust gap 15.3% vs NAP-Tuning gap 24.2%, but TeCoA clean accuracy collapses 69.1%->36.8% (-32.3pp)
Training time on Pets dataset (Table IV): AdvPT 34 min (1.00x), AdvPT-V 91 min (2.68x), FAP 91 min (2.68x), APD 95 min (2.79x), NAP-Tuning 99 min (2.91x), TeCoA 123 min (3.62x)
NAP-Tuning + DiffPure vs standalone NAP-Tuning: +12.9 percentage points average improvement across 11 datasets
TokenRefiner ablation: removing module (0 layers) causes training collapse (near-random accuracy) under 5-step PGD training; 2 layers resolves instability and is adopted as default
